description: NeetCode Core Skills SQL — 50 problems ordered by progression. Categories: Basic SELECT → JOINs → Aggregation → Subqueries → Window Functions → CTEs → Advanced. Plus DB Concepts for system design interviews.
schedule: 2 problems per session, daily from week 1, ~20 min

problems.title: Combine Two Tables
problems.description: Report firstName, lastName, city, state for every person. Include persons even if no address exists.
problems.key_concept: LEFT JOIN — include all rows from left table even when no matching row in right table. NULL for missing right-side columns.

hints.L1: You need all persons, even those without an address. Which JOIN preserves all rows from Person?
hints.L2: LEFT JOIN Person ON Address.personId = Person.personId. All Person rows kept; Address columns are NULL when no match.
hints.L3: SELECT p.firstName, p.lastName, a.city, a.state FROM Person p LEFT JOIN Address a ON p.personId = a.personId

problems.title: Duplicate Emails
problems.description: Find all emails that appear more than once in the Person table.
problems.key_concept: GROUP BY + HAVING COUNT > 1 to find duplicates. HAVING filters aggregated groups.

hints.L1: Group by email and count occurrences. Filter groups where count > 1.
hints.L2: SELECT email FROM Person GROUP BY email HAVING COUNT(*) > 1.
hints.L3: SELECT email FROM Person GROUP BY email HAVING COUNT(email) > 1

problems.optimal_solution: SELECT email FROM Person GROUP BY email HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

problems.title: Customers Without Orders
problems.description: Find customers who never placed an order.
problems.key_concept: NOT IN subquery OR LEFT JOIN + IS NULL. Anti-join pattern.

hints.L1: Find customers whose ID doesn't appear in the Orders table.
hints.L2: Option 1: SELECT name FROM Customers WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT customerId FROM Orders). Option 2: LEFT JOIN and filter NULL.
hints.L3: SELECT c.name AS Customers FROM Customers c LEFT JOIN Orders o ON c.id = o.customerId WHERE o.customerId IS NULL

problems.optimal_solution:
SELECT c.name AS Customers
FROM Customers c
LEFT JOIN Orders o ON c.id = o.customerId
WHERE o.customerId IS NULL;

problems.title: Calculate Special Bonus
problems.description: Calculate bonus for each employee: bonus = salary if employee_id is odd AND name doesn't start with 'M', else 0.
problems.key_concept: CASE WHEN for conditional columns. Modulo (%) for odd/even check. String functions: LIKE.

hints.L1: Use CASE WHEN to return salary or 0 based on two conditions joined by AND.
hints.L2: CASE WHEN employee_id % 2 = 1 AND name NOT LIKE 'M%' THEN salary ELSE 0 END
hints.L3: SELECT employee_id, CASE WHEN employee_id % 2 <> 0 AND name NOT LIKE 'M%' THEN salary ELSE 0 END AS bonus FROM Employees ORDER BY employee_id

problems.optimal_solution:
SELECT employee_id,
  CASE WHEN employee_id % 2 <> 0 AND name NOT LIKE 'M%' THEN salary ELSE 0 END AS bonus
FROM Employees
ORDER BY employee_id;

problems.title: Customers With Positive Revenue
problems.description: Find customers who had positive revenue in year 2021.
problems.key_concept: Simple WHERE filter on multiple conditions.

hints.L1: Filter the Customers table for year = 2021 and revenue > 0.
hints.L2: SELECT customer_id FROM Customers WHERE year = 2021 AND revenue > 0.
hints.L3: SELECT customer_id FROM Customers WHERE year = 2021 AND revenue > 0

problems.optimal_solution: SELECT customer_id FROM Customers WHERE year = 2021 AND revenue > 0;

problems.title: Sales Person
problems.description: Find salespeople who did NOT have any orders related to the company named 'RED'.
problems.key_concept: NOT IN with multi-table subquery. Find set to exclude, then negate.

hints.L1: First find all salesperson IDs who have orders with 'RED'. Then exclude them from SalesPerson.
hints.L2: Subquery: SELECT DISTINCT sales_id FROM Orders o JOIN Company c ON o.com_id = c.com_id WHERE c.name = 'RED'
hints.L3: SELECT name FROM SalesPerson WHERE sales_id NOT IN (SELECT DISTINCT o.sales_id FROM Orders o JOIN Company c ON o.com_id = c.com_id WHERE c.name = 'RED')

problems.title: Sellers With No Sales
problems.description: Find sellers who had no sales in year 2020.
problems.key_concept: LEFT JOIN with date filter IN JOIN condition (not WHERE) to preserve NULL rows for no-match detection.

hints.L1: LEFT JOIN Orders on seller_id where order_date is in 2020. WHERE order_id IS NULL means no 2020 sales.
hints.L2: Put the year filter in the JOIN condition, NOT the WHERE clause — otherwise NULL rows get filtered out.
hints.L3: SELECT s.seller_name FROM Seller s LEFT JOIN Orders o ON s.seller_id = o.seller_id AND YEAR(o.sale_date) = 2020 WHERE o.order_id IS NULL ORDER BY s.seller_name

problems.optimal_solution:
SELECT s.seller_name FROM Seller s
LEFT JOIN Orders o ON s.seller_id = o.seller_id AND YEAR(o.sale_date) = 2020
WHERE o.order_id IS NULL
ORDER BY s.seller_name;

problems.title: Top Travellers
problems.description: Report name and total distance travelled for each user. Users who never travelled count as 0.
problems.key_concept: LEFT JOIN to include users with no trips + IFNULL for NULL sum. GROUP BY + ORDER BY multiple columns.

hints.L1: LEFT JOIN to include users with no rides. SUM(distance) with IFNULL(..., 0).
hints.L2: GROUP BY u.id, ORDER BY SUM(distance) DESC, u.name ASC
hints.L3: SELECT u.name, IFNULL(SUM(r.distance), 0) AS travelled_distance FROM Users u LEFT JOIN Rides r ON u.id = r.user_id GROUP BY u.id ORDER BY travelled_distance DESC, u.name ASC

problems.optimal_solution:
SELECT u.name, IFNULL(SUM(r.distance), 0) AS travelled_distance
FROM Users u LEFT JOIN Rides r ON u.id = r.user_id
GROUP BY u.id
ORDER BY travelled_distance DESC, u.name ASC;

problems.title: Game Play Analysis I
problems.description: Find the first login date for each player.
problems.key_concept: MIN() aggregation with GROUP BY. First occurrence per group.

hints.L1: Group by player and find the minimum event_date.
hints.L2: SELECT player_id, MIN(event_date) AS first_login FROM Activity GROUP BY player_id
hints.L3: SELECT player_id, MIN(event_date) AS first_login FROM Activity GROUP BY player_id

problems.optimal_solution: SELECT player_id, MIN(event_date) AS first_login FROM Activity GROUP BY player_id;

problems.title: Warehouse Manager
problems.description: Calculate total cubic footage stored in each warehouse (volume = units × Width × Length × Height).
problems.key_concept: SUM of expression (multi-column multiplication). JOIN then aggregate.

hints.L1: Join Warehouse with Products. Volume per row = units * product dimensions. SUM per warehouse.
hints.L2: SELECT w.name, SUM(w.units * p.Width * p.Length * p.Height) AS volume FROM Warehouse w JOIN Products p ON w.product_id = p.product_id GROUP BY w.name
hints.L3: SELECT w.name AS warehouse_name, SUM(w.units * p.Width * p.Length * p.Height) AS volume FROM Warehouse w JOIN Products p ON w.product_id = p.product_id GROUP BY w.name

problems.title: Customer Placing the Largest Number of Orders
problems.description: Find the customer with the most orders.
problems.key_concept: GROUP BY + ORDER BY COUNT DESC + LIMIT 1. Top-1 pattern.

hints.L1: Count orders per customer, sort descending, take the top 1.
hints.L2: SELECT customer_number FROM Orders GROUP BY customer_number ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC LIMIT 1
hints.L3: SELECT customer_number FROM Orders GROUP BY customer_number ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC LIMIT 1

problems.optimal_solution: SELECT customer_number FROM Orders GROUP BY customer_number ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC LIMIT 1;

problems.title: Find Total Time Spent by Each Employee
problems.description: Calculate total time (in minutes) each employee spent in the office each day.
problems.key_concept: SUM of expression (column subtraction). GROUP BY multiple columns.

hints.L1: Time per entry = out_time - in_time. Sum per employee per day.
hints.L2: SELECT event_day AS day, emp_id, SUM(out_time - in_time) AS total_time FROM Employees GROUP BY event_day, emp_id
hints.L3: SELECT event_day AS day, emp_id, SUM(out_time - in_time) AS total_time FROM Employees GROUP BY event_day, emp_id

problems.optimal_solution: SELECT event_day AS day, emp_id, SUM(out_time - in_time) AS total_time FROM Employees GROUP BY event_day, emp_id;

problems.title: Immediate Food Delivery I
problems.description: Find what percentage of orders are 'immediate' (preferred_delivery_date = order_date). Round to 2 decimal places.
problems.key_concept: AVG of boolean expression * 100 = percentage. Or SUM(CASE WHEN...)/COUNT * 100.

hints.L1: Count immediate orders and divide by total, multiply by 100.
hints.L2: ROUND(100 * AVG(order_date = preferred_delivery_date), 2) — in MySQL, boolean comparison returns 0/1.
hints.L3: SELECT ROUND(100 * SUM(CASE WHEN order_date = preferred_delivery_date THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*), 2) AS immediate_percentage FROM Delivery

problems.optimal_solution: SELECT ROUND(100 * AVG(order_date = preferred_delivery_date), 2) AS immediate_percentage FROM Delivery;

problems.title: Bank Account Summary II
problems.description: Find account names and balances for accounts with total balance > 10000.
problems.key_concept: JOIN + GROUP BY + HAVING SUM() > threshold.

hints.L1: Join Users and Transactions, group by account, sum amounts, filter sum > 10000.
hints.L2: JOIN on account → GROUP BY u.name → HAVING SUM(amount) > 10000
hints.L3: SELECT u.name, SUM(t.amount) AS balance FROM Users u JOIN Transactions t ON u.account = t.account GROUP BY u.account HAVING balance > 10000

problems.title: Actors and Directors Who Cooperated At Least Three Times
problems.description: Find all (actor_id, director_id) pairs who worked together at least 3 times.
problems.key_concept: GROUP BY multiple columns + HAVING COUNT >= N.

hints.L1: Group by both actor_id and director_id together. Filter pairs with count >= 3.
hints.L2: SELECT actor_id, director_id FROM ActorDirector GROUP BY actor_id, director_id HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3
hints.L3: SELECT actor_id, director_id FROM ActorDirector GROUP BY actor_id, director_id HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3

problems.optimal_solution: SELECT actor_id, director_id FROM ActorDirector GROUP BY actor_id, director_id HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3;

problems.title: Daily Leads and Partners
problems.description: For each date and make, count unique leads and unique partners.
problems.key_concept: COUNT(DISTINCT col) — count unique values within each group.

hints.L1: GROUP BY date_id and make_name. Use COUNT(DISTINCT lead_id) and COUNT(DISTINCT partner_id).
hints.L2: SELECT date_id, make_name, COUNT(DISTINCT lead_id) AS unique_leads, COUNT(DISTINCT partner_id) AS unique_partners FROM DailySales GROUP BY date_id, make_name
hints.L3: SELECT date_id, make_name, COUNT(DISTINCT lead_id) AS unique_leads, COUNT(DISTINCT partner_id) AS unique_partners FROM DailySales GROUP BY date_id, make_name

problems.optimal_solution: SELECT date_id, make_name, COUNT(DISTINCT lead_id) AS unique_leads, COUNT(DISTINCT partner_id) AS unique_partners FROM DailySales GROUP BY date_id, make_name;

problems.title: Find the Team Size
problems.description: For each employee, find the size of the team they belong to.
problems.key_concept: Subquery count per group, joined back to each row.

hints.L1: Count employees per team. Join that count back to each employee row.
hints.L2: Subquery: SELECT team_id, COUNT(*) AS team_size FROM Employee GROUP BY team_id. Then join to Employee on team_id.
hints.L3: SELECT e.employee_id, t.team_size FROM Employee e JOIN (SELECT team_id, COUNT(*) AS team_size FROM Employee GROUP BY team_id) t ON e.team_id = t.team_id

problems.title: Friendly Movies Streamed Last Month
problems.description: Find titles of movies rated for kids that were streamed in October 2020.
problems.key_concept: JOIN + multiple WHERE filters (category and date range).

hints.L1: Join TVProgram with Content on content_id. Filter where Kids_content = 'Y' and October 2020.
hints.L2: WHERE c.Kids_content = 'Y' AND MONTH(t.program_date) = 10 AND YEAR(t.program_date) = 2020
hints.L3: SELECT DISTINCT c.title FROM TVProgram t JOIN Content c ON t.content_id = c.content_id WHERE c.Kids_content = 'Y' AND YEAR(t.program_date) = 2020 AND MONTH(t.program_date) = 10

problems.title: Students With Invalid Departments
problems.description: Find students whose department_id does not exist in the Departments table.
problems.key_concept: NOT IN subquery or LEFT JOIN + IS NULL. Anti-join.

hints.L1: LEFT JOIN Departments. Students with no matching department will have NULL for department columns.
hints.L2: SELECT s.id, s.name FROM Students s LEFT JOIN Departments d ON s.department_id = d.id WHERE d.id IS NULL
hints.L3: SELECT id, name FROM Students WHERE department_id NOT IN (SELECT id FROM Departments)

problems.optimal_solution: SELECT id, name FROM Students WHERE department_id NOT IN (SELECT id FROM Departments);

problems.title: Employees With Missing Information
problems.description: Find employee IDs who are in either Employees or Salaries table but not both.
problems.key_concept: FULL OUTER JOIN via UNION of two LEFT JOINs in MySQL.

hints.L1: You need IDs that exist in one table but not the other. MySQL lacks FULL OUTER JOIN — use UNION.
hints.L2: NOT IN subquery for each direction.
hints.L3: SELECT employee_id FROM Employees WHERE employee_id NOT IN (SELECT employee_id FROM Salaries) UNION SELECT employee_id FROM Salaries WHERE employee_id NOT IN (SELECT employee_id FROM Employees) ORDER BY employee_id

problems.optimal_solution:
SELECT employee_id FROM Employees WHERE employee_id NOT IN (SELECT employee_id FROM Salaries)
UNION
SELECT employee_id FROM Salaries WHERE employee_id NOT IN (SELECT employee_id FROM Employees)
ORDER BY employee_id;

problems.title: Neetflex Banned Accounts
problems.description: Find account IDs that are active in the Accounts table but marked as banned in the BannedAccounts table.
problems.key_concept: Simple INNER JOIN or IN subquery to find matching IDs between two tables.

hints.L1: Find account_ids that appear in both tables.
hints.L2: SELECT account_id FROM Accounts WHERE account_id IN (SELECT account_id FROM BannedAccounts)
hints.L3: SELECT a.account_id FROM Accounts a JOIN BannedAccounts b ON a.account_id = b.account_id

problems.optimal_solution: SELECT account_id FROM Accounts WHERE account_id IN (SELECT account_id FROM BannedAccounts);

problems.title: Consecutive Available Seats
problems.description: Find all seats that are free and consecutive (adjacent seat_id values, both free).
problems.key_concept: Self-join on same table with adjacent row condition: ABS(c1.seat_id - c2.seat_id) = 1. Both must be free.

hints.L1: Self-join the cinema table to find adjacent seats. Both must have free = 1.
hints.L2: JOIN Cinema c2 ON ABS(c1.seat_id - c2.seat_id) = 1 WHERE c1.free = 1 AND c2.free = 1.
hints.L3: SELECT DISTINCT c1.seat_id FROM Cinema c1 JOIN Cinema c2 ON ABS(c1.seat_id - c2.seat_id) = 1 WHERE c1.free = 1 AND c2.free = 1 ORDER BY c1.seat_id

problems.title: Rearrange Products Table
problems.description: Unpivot a wide products table (product_id, store1, store2, store3) to long format (product_id, store, price).
problems.key_concept: UNION ALL to unpivot wide format to long format. One SELECT per column. Filter NULL prices.

hints.L1: Write one SELECT per store column. UNION ALL them together.
hints.L2: SELECT product_id, 'store1' AS store, store1 AS price FROM Products WHERE store1 IS NOT NULL UNION ALL ...
hints.L3: SELECT product_id, 'store1' AS store, store1 AS price FROM Products WHERE store1 IS NOT NULL UNION ALL SELECT product_id, 'store2', store2 FROM Products WHERE store2 IS NOT NULL UNION ALL SELECT product_id, 'store3', store3 FROM Products WHERE store3 IS NOT NULL

problems.title: Shortest Distance in a Line
problems.description: Find the shortest distance between any two points on a line.
problems.key_concept: Self-join on p1.x < p2.x, compute p2.x - p1.x for all pairs, take MIN.

hints.L1: Self-join and compute differences. Use x < other_x to avoid duplicates.
hints.L2: SELECT MIN(p2.x - p1.x) AS shortest FROM Point p1 JOIN Point p2 ON p1.x < p2.x
hints.L3: SELECT MIN(p2.x - p1.x) AS shortest FROM Point p1 JOIN Point p2 ON p2.x > p1.x

problems.optimal_solution: SELECT MIN(p2.x - p1.x) AS shortest FROM Point p1 JOIN Point p2 ON p2.x > p1.x;

problems.title: Customers Who Bought A and B but Not C
problems.description: Find customers who bought products A and B but not product C.
problems.key_concept: Conditional aggregation: HAVING SUM(product='A')>0 AND SUM(product='B')>0 AND SUM(product='C')=0.

hints.L1: Use HAVING with SUM(CASE WHEN ...) to check which products each customer bought.
hints.L2: GROUP BY customer_id. HAVING SUM(product_name='A')>0 AND SUM(product_name='B')>0 AND SUM(product_name='C')=0
hints.L3: SELECT c.customer_id, c.customer_name FROM Customers c JOIN Orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id GROUP BY c.customer_id HAVING SUM(o.product_name = 'A') > 0 AND SUM(o.product_name = 'B') > 0 AND SUM(o.product_name = 'C') = 0

problems.optimal_solution:
SELECT c.customer_id, c.customer_name FROM Customers c
JOIN Orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
GROUP BY c.customer_id
HAVING SUM(o.product_name = 'A') > 0 AND SUM(o.product_name = 'B') > 0 AND SUM(o.product_name = 'C') = 0;

problems.title: Highest Grade For Each Student
problems.description: For each student, find the course with the highest grade. If tie, pick the lowest course_id.
problems.key_concept: Filter with max subquery, then MIN(course_id) for tie-breaking.

hints.L1: First find max grade per student. Then find the minimum course_id for rows matching that max.
hints.L2: Use correlated subquery or rank with PARTITION BY student_id ORDER BY grade DESC, course_id ASC then filter rank=1.
hints.L3: SELECT student_id, MIN(course_id) AS course_id, grade FROM Enrollments WHERE (student_id, grade) IN (SELECT student_id, MAX(grade) FROM Enrollments GROUP BY student_id) GROUP BY student_id, grade ORDER BY student_id

problems.optimal_solution:
SELECT student_id, MIN(course_id) AS course_id, grade
FROM Enrollments
WHERE (student_id, grade) IN (SELECT student_id, MAX(grade) FROM Enrollments GROUP BY student_id)
GROUP BY student_id, grade ORDER BY student_id;

problems.title: Apples & Oranges
problems.description: For each sale_date, find the difference between number of apples and oranges sold.
problems.key_concept: SUM(CASE WHEN ...) - SUM(CASE WHEN ...) or SUM with signed values.

hints.L1: For each date, you need counts of apples and oranges. Use conditional SUM.
hints.L2: SUM(CASE WHEN item = 'apples' THEN sold_num ELSE -sold_num END)
hints.L3: SELECT sale_date, SUM(CASE WHEN item='apples' THEN sold_num ELSE -sold_num END) AS diff FROM Sales GROUP BY sale_date ORDER BY sale_date

problems.optimal_solution:
SELECT sale_date, SUM(CASE WHEN item = 'apples' THEN sold_num ELSE -sold_num END) AS diff
FROM Sales GROUP BY sale_date ORDER BY sale_date;

problems.title: Number of Calls Between Two Persons
problems.description: For each unique pair (person1, person2), return total calls and duration. Normalize so person1 < person2.
problems.key_concept: LEAST/GREATEST to normalize direction, then GROUP BY normalized pair.

hints.L1: Normalize (caller, recipient) pairs using LEAST/GREATEST regardless of direction.
hints.L2: SELECT LEAST(from_id,to_id) AS person1, GREATEST(from_id,to_id) AS person2, COUNT(*) AS call_count, SUM(duration) AS total_duration FROM Calls GROUP BY person1, person2
hints.L3: SELECT LEAST(from_id,to_id) AS person1, GREATEST(from_id,to_id) AS person2, COUNT(*) AS call_count, SUM(duration) AS total_duration FROM Calls GROUP BY 1,2

problems.title: Customer Order Frequency
problems.description: Find customers who spent at least $100 in June 2020 AND at least $100 in July 2020.
problems.key_concept: HAVING with two conditional SUM conditions on different date ranges.

hints.L1: Join customers with orders and products. Filter to 2020. Group by customer, check spending by month.
hints.L2: HAVING SUM(IF(MONTH(order_date)=6, quantity*price, 0)) >= 100 AND SUM(IF(MONTH(order_date)=7, ...))
hints.L3: SELECT c.customer_id, c.name FROM Customers c JOIN Orders o ON c.customer_id=o.customer_id JOIN Product p ON o.product_id=p.product_id WHERE YEAR(o.order_date)=2020 GROUP BY c.customer_id HAVING SUM(IF(MONTH(o.order_date)=6, o.quantity*p.price, 0))>=100 AND SUM(IF(MONTH(o.order_date)=7, o.quantity*p.price, 0))>=100

problems.optimal_solution:
SELECT c.customer_id, c.name FROM Customers c
JOIN Orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
JOIN Product p ON o.product_id = p.product_id
WHERE YEAR(o.order_date) = 2020
GROUP BY c.customer_id
HAVING SUM(IF(MONTH(o.order_date)=6, o.quantity*p.price, 0)) >= 100
   AND SUM(IF(MONTH(o.order_date)=7, o.quantity*p.price, 0)) >= 100;

problems.title: Countries You Can Safely Invest In
problems.description: Find countries where average call duration exceeds the global average.
problems.key_concept: Multi-table join + HAVING AVG > (scalar subquery for global average).

hints.L1: Get each country's average call duration. Compare to global average (subquery returning one number).
hints.L2: HAVING AVG(duration) > (SELECT AVG(duration) FROM Calls).
hints.L3: SELECT co.name AS country FROM Person p JOIN Calls c ON p.id IN (c.caller_id, c.callee_id) JOIN Country co ON LEFT(p.phone_number,3) = co.country_code GROUP BY co.name HAVING AVG(c.duration) > (SELECT AVG(duration) FROM Calls)

problems.optimal_solution:
SELECT co.name AS country
FROM Person p
JOIN Calls c ON p.id IN (c.caller_id, c.callee_id)
JOIN Country co ON LEFT(p.phone_number, 3) = co.country_code
GROUP BY co.name
HAVING AVG(c.duration) > (SELECT AVG(duration) FROM Calls);

problems.title: Page Recommendations
problems.description: Recommend pages to user_id=1 that are liked by friends of user 1 but not already liked by user 1.
problems.key_concept: Multi-step: find friends (bidirectional), find pages liked by friends, exclude already liked pages.

hints.L1: Step 1: find all friends of user 1 (bidirectional). Step 2: pages liked by those friends. Step 3: exclude pages already liked by user 1.
hints.L2: Friends: UNION of (user2_id WHERE user1_id=1) and (user1_id WHERE user2_id=1).
hints.L3: SELECT DISTINCT l.page_id AS recommended_page FROM Likes l WHERE l.user_id IN (SELECT user2_id FROM Friendship WHERE user1_id=1 UNION SELECT user1_id FROM Friendship WHERE user2_id=1) AND l.page_id NOT IN (SELECT page_id FROM Likes WHERE user_id=1)

problems.optimal_solution:
SELECT DISTINCT l.page_id AS recommended_page FROM Likes l
WHERE l.user_id IN (
  SELECT user2_id FROM Friendship WHERE user1_id = 1
  UNION
  SELECT user1_id FROM Friendship WHERE user2_id = 1
)
AND l.page_id NOT IN (SELECT page_id FROM Likes WHERE user_id = 1);

problems.title: Tree Node
problems.description: Classify each node as Root, Inner, or Leaf based on parent relationships.
problems.key_concept: CASE WHEN with subqueries: Root (p_id IS NULL), Leaf (not in any p_id), Inner (rest).

hints.L1: Root: p_id is NULL. Leaf: id does not appear as any node's p_id. Inner: everything else.
hints.L2: CASE WHEN p_id IS NULL THEN 'Root' WHEN id NOT IN (SELECT p_id WHERE p_id IS NOT NULL) THEN 'Leaf' ELSE 'Inner' END
hints.L3: SELECT id, CASE WHEN p_id IS NULL THEN 'Root' WHEN id IN (SELECT DISTINCT p_id FROM Tree WHERE p_id IS NOT NULL) THEN 'Inner' ELSE 'Leaf' END AS type FROM Tree

problems.optimal_solution:
SELECT id,
  CASE WHEN p_id IS NULL THEN 'Root'
       WHEN id IN (SELECT DISTINCT p_id FROM Tree WHERE p_id IS NOT NULL) THEN 'Inner'
       ELSE 'Leaf' END AS type
FROM Tree;

problems.title: Game Play Analysis II
problems.description: Find the device_id each player first logged in from.
problems.key_concept: Correlated subquery or ROW_NUMBER PARTITION BY player ORDER BY date — pick row 1.

hints.L1: For each player, you need the device from their earliest login.
hints.L2: WHERE event_date = (SELECT MIN(event_date) FROM Activity WHERE player_id = a.player_id).
hints.L3: SELECT player_id, device_id FROM Activity WHERE (player_id, event_date) IN (SELECT player_id, MIN(event_date) FROM Activity GROUP BY player_id)

problems.optimal_solution:
SELECT player_id, device_id FROM Activity
WHERE (player_id, event_date) IN (SELECT player_id, MIN(event_date) FROM Activity GROUP BY player_id);

problems.title: Game Play Analysis III
problems.description: For each player and each date, find the running total of games played.
problems.key_concept: SUM() OVER (PARTITION BY player_id ORDER BY event_date) — cumulative sum window function.

hints.L1: You need a running total per player ordered by date. Use a window function.
hints.L2: SUM(games_played) OVER (PARTITION BY player_id ORDER BY event_date). Default frame includes all preceding rows.
hints.L3: SELECT player_id, event_date, SUM(games_played) OVER (PARTITION BY player_id ORDER BY event_date) AS games_played_so_far FROM Activity

problems.title: Department Highest Salary
problems.description: Find employees who earn the highest salary in their department.
problems.key_concept: Correlated subquery with MAX() or window RANK() PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC.

hints.L1: For each employee, check if their salary equals the max in their department.
hints.L2: WHERE (e.departmentId, e.salary) IN (SELECT departmentId, MAX(salary) FROM Employee GROUP BY departmentId).
hints.L3: SELECT d.name AS Department, e.name AS Employee, e.salary FROM Employee e JOIN Department d ON e.departmentId = d.id WHERE (e.departmentId, e.salary) IN (SELECT departmentId, MAX(salary) FROM Employee GROUP BY departmentId)

problems.title: The Latest Login in 2020
problems.description: Find the latest login for each user in year 2020.
problems.key_concept: MAX() GROUP BY with date filter.

hints.L1: Filter to 2020, group by user_id, take MAX(time_stamp).
hints.L2: SELECT user_id, MAX(time_stamp) AS last_stamp FROM Logins WHERE YEAR(time_stamp) = 2020 GROUP BY user_id
hints.L3: SELECT user_id, MAX(time_stamp) AS last_stamp FROM Logins WHERE YEAR(time_stamp) = 2020 GROUP BY user_id

problems.optimal_solution: SELECT user_id, MAX(time_stamp) AS last_stamp FROM Logins WHERE YEAR(time_stamp) = 2020 GROUP BY user_id;

problems.title: Grand Slam Titles
problems.description: Count how many Grand Slam titles each player won across 4 tournament columns.
problems.key_concept: UNION ALL to unpivot tournament columns, then COUNT per player.

hints.L1: The Championships table has 4 tournament columns. UNION ALL to create one row per player per title.
hints.L2: SELECT wimbledon AS player_id FROM Championships UNION ALL SELECT Fr_open UNION ALL SELECT US_open UNION ALL SELECT Au_open. Then GROUP BY COUNT.
hints.L3: SELECT p.player_id, p.player_name, COUNT(*) AS grand_slams_count FROM Players p JOIN (SELECT wimbledon as id FROM Championships UNION ALL SELECT Fr_open UNION ALL SELECT US_open UNION ALL SELECT Au_open) t ON p.player_id = t.id GROUP BY p.player_id

problems.title: The Most Recent Orders for Each Product
problems.description: Find the most recent order(s) for each product. Include all if tied on date.
problems.key_concept: Filter with (product_id, order_date) IN (SELECT product_id, MAX(order_date) ...).

hints.L1: For each product, find the maximum order_date. Return all orders matching that date.
hints.L2: (o.product_id, o.order_date) IN (SELECT product_id, MAX(order_date) FROM Orders GROUP BY product_id)
hints.L3: SELECT p.product_name, o.product_id, o.order_id, o.order_date FROM Orders o JOIN Products p ON o.product_id = p.product_id WHERE (o.product_id, o.order_date) IN (SELECT product_id, MAX(order_date) FROM Orders GROUP BY product_id) ORDER BY p.product_name, o.product_id, o.order_id

problems.optimal_solution:
SELECT p.product_name, o.product_id, o.order_id, o.order_date
FROM Orders o JOIN Products p ON o.product_id = p.product_id
WHERE (o.product_id, o.order_date) IN (SELECT product_id, MAX(order_date) FROM Orders GROUP BY product_id)
ORDER BY p.product_name, o.product_id, o.order_id;

problems.title: The Most Recent Three Orders
problems.description: Find the three most recent orders for each customer.
problems.key_concept: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date DESC) <= 3.

hints.L1: Assign row numbers per customer ordered by date descending. Keep rows 1, 2, 3.
hints.L2: WITH ranked AS (SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date DESC) AS rn FROM Orders) SELECT ... WHERE rn <= 3
hints.L3: SELECT c.name AS customer_name, o.customer_id, o.order_id, o.order_date FROM (SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date DESC) AS rn FROM Orders) o JOIN Customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id WHERE o.rn <= 3

problems.optimal_solution:
SELECT c.name AS customer_name, o.customer_id, o.order_id, o.order_date
FROM (SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date DESC) AS rn FROM Orders) o
JOIN Customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id
WHERE o.rn <= 3 ORDER BY customer_name, o.customer_id, o.order_date DESC;

problems.title: Maximum Transaction Each Day
problems.description: Find transaction(s) with the maximum amount for each day. Include ties.
problems.key_concept: Filter with (DATE(day), amount) IN (SELECT DATE(day), MAX(amount) ...).

hints.L1: For each day, find max amount. Return all transactions matching that max.
hints.L2: WHERE (DATE(day), amount) IN (SELECT DATE(day), MAX(amount) FROM Transactions GROUP BY DATE(day))
hints.L3: SELECT transaction_id FROM Transactions WHERE (DATE(day), amount) IN (SELECT DATE(day), MAX(amount) FROM Transactions GROUP BY DATE(day)) ORDER BY transaction_id

problems.title: Evaluate Boolean Expression
problems.description: Evaluate expressions stored as rows. Each row has left_operand, operator, right_operand. Look up variable values and evaluate.
problems.key_concept: Multi-table JOIN with CASE WHEN to evaluate operators. Join Variables twice for left and right.

hints.L1: Join Variables twice (for left and right operand) to get their values. Use CASE WHEN operator='>' ...
hints.L2: JOIN Variables lv ON e.left_operand = lv.name JOIN Variables rv ON e.right_operand = rv.name. CASE WHEN ...
hints.L3: SELECT e.left_operand, e.operator, e.right_operand, CASE WHEN e.operator='>' AND lv.value>rv.value THEN 'true' WHEN e.operator='<' AND lv.value<rv.value THEN 'true' WHEN e.operator='=' AND lv.value=rv.value THEN 'true' ELSE 'false' END AS value FROM Expressions e JOIN Variables lv ON e.left_operand=lv.name JOIN Variables rv ON e.right_operand=rv.name

problems.title: Team Scores in Football Tournament
problems.description: Calculate total points for each team (win=3, draw=1, loss=0).
problems.key_concept: UNION ALL to expand match results to one row per team, then conditional SUM.

hints.L1: Each match has home and away team. UNION ALL to create one row per team per match.
hints.L2: CASE WHEN host_goals > guest_goals THEN 3 WHEN equal THEN 1 ELSE 0. Symmetric for away team.
hints.L3: SELECT t.team_id, t.team_name, COALESCE(SUM(pts),0) AS num_points FROM Teams t LEFT JOIN (SELECT host_team id, CASE WHEN host_goals>guest_goals THEN 3 WHEN host_goals=guest_goals THEN 1 ELSE 0 END pts FROM Matches UNION ALL SELECT guest_team, CASE WHEN guest_goals>host_goals THEN 3 WHEN guest_goals=host_goals THEN 1 ELSE 0 END FROM Matches) p ON t.team_id=p.id GROUP BY t.team_id ORDER BY num_points DESC, t.team_id

problems.optimal_solution:
SELECT t.team_id, t.team_name, COALESCE(SUM(p.pts), 0) AS num_points
FROM Teams t LEFT JOIN (
  SELECT host_team AS id, CASE WHEN host_goals>guest_goals THEN 3 WHEN host_goals=guest_goals THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS pts FROM Matches
  UNION ALL
  SELECT guest_team, CASE WHEN guest_goals>host_goals THEN 3 WHEN guest_goals=host_goals THEN 1 ELSE 0 END FROM Matches
) p ON t.team_id = p.id
GROUP BY t.team_id ORDER BY num_points DESC, t.team_id;

problems.title: The Most Frequently Ordered Products for Each Customer
problems.description: Find the most frequently ordered product(s) for each customer.
problems.key_concept: RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY customer ORDER BY COUNT DESC) = 1. Handles ties.

hints.L1: Count orders per (customer, product). Rank within customer by count descending. Keep rank = 1.
hints.L2: WITH cte AS (SELECT customer_id, product_id, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC) AS rk FROM Orders GROUP BY customer_id, product_id)
hints.L3: WITH cte AS (SELECT customer_id, product_id, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC) AS rk FROM Orders GROUP BY customer_id, product_id) SELECT c.customer_id, c.product_id, p.product_name FROM cte c JOIN Products p ON c.product_id=p.product_id WHERE c.rk=1

problems.title: Biggest Window Between Visits
problems.description: For each user, find the largest window (in days) between consecutive visits, including to 2021-01-01.
problems.key_concept: LEAD() window function with sentinel date. DATEDIFF between current and next visit.

hints.L1: Use LEAD to get next visit_date per user. Compute gap. Include gap from last visit to 2021-01-01.
hints.L2: LEAD(visit_date, 1, '2021-01-01') OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY visit_date) gives next date.
hints.L3: WITH t AS (SELECT user_id, visit_date, LEAD(visit_date, 1, '2021-01-01') OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY visit_date) AS next_visit FROM UserVisits) SELECT user_id, MAX(DATEDIFF(next_visit, visit_date)) AS biggest_window FROM t GROUP BY user_id

problems.title: Project Employees III
problems.description: Find employees with the most experience for each project. Include ties.
problems.key_concept: Filter with (project_id, experience_years) IN (SELECT project_id, MAX(experience_years) ...).

hints.L1: For each project, find the maximum experience. Return all employees with that max.
hints.L2: (p.project_id, e.experience_years) IN (SELECT project_id, MAX(exp) from joined table GROUP BY project_id)
hints.L3: SELECT p.project_id, p.employee_id FROM Project p JOIN Employee e ON p.employee_id = e.employee_id WHERE (p.project_id, e.experience_years) IN (SELECT p2.project_id, MAX(e2.experience_years) FROM Project p2 JOIN Employee e2 ON p2.employee_id = e2.employee_id GROUP BY p2.project_id)

problems.title: Find the Start and End Number of Continuous Ranges
problems.description: Given sorted log_ids, find the start and end of each continuous range.
problems.key_concept: Gaps-and-islands: log_id - ROW_NUMBER() gives a constant for consecutive sequences. Group by this key.

hints.L1: Consecutive integers form islands. Subtract ROW_NUMBER from value to get a group identifier.
hints.L2: grouping_key = log_id - ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY log_id). Same island = same group key.
hints.L3: WITH t AS (SELECT log_id, log_id - ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY log_id) AS grp FROM Logs) SELECT MIN(log_id) AS start_id, MAX(log_id) AS end_id FROM t GROUP BY grp ORDER BY start_id

problems.optimal_solution:
WITH t AS (SELECT log_id, log_id - ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY log_id) AS grp FROM Logs)
SELECT MIN(log_id) AS start_id, MAX(log_id) AS end_id FROM t GROUP BY grp ORDER BY start_id;

problems.title: All People Report to the Given Manager
problems.description: Find all employee IDs who directly or indirectly report to manager_id = 1.
problems.key_concept: Recursive CTE to traverse management hierarchy. Base: direct reports. Recursive: their reports.

hints.L1: This is a tree traversal. Use a recursive CTE.
hints.L2: Anchor: SELECT employee_id FROM Employees WHERE manager_id = 1 AND employee_id != 1. Recursive: JOIN on manager_id = cte.employee_id.
hints.L3: WITH RECURSIVE cte AS (SELECT employee_id FROM Employees WHERE manager_id = 1 AND employee_id != 1 UNION ALL SELECT e.employee_id FROM Employees e JOIN cte ON e.manager_id = cte.employee_id WHERE e.employee_id != 1) SELECT employee_id FROM cte

problems.title: Find the Quiet Students in All Exams
problems.description: Find students who never scored the highest or lowest in any exam they took.
problems.key_concept: Exclude students who ever appear as max or min scorer in any exam they attended.

hints.L1: Find students who ever scored the max or min in any exam. Exclude them from all students who took exams.
hints.L2: Get max/min per exam. Find student_ids matching those scores in their respective exams. Negate.
hints.L3: WITH exam_stats AS (SELECT exam_id, MAX(score) max_s, MIN(score) min_s FROM Exam GROUP BY exam_id) SELECT DISTINCT s.student_id, s.student_name FROM Student s JOIN Exam e ON s.student_id=e.student_id WHERE s.student_id NOT IN (SELECT e2.student_id FROM Exam e2 JOIN exam_stats es ON e2.exam_id=es.exam_id WHERE e2.score=es.max_s OR e2.score=es.min_s) ORDER BY s.student_id

problems.title: Find the Subtasks That Did Not Execute
problems.description: Given Tasks with subtasks_count and Executed with completed subtasks, find (task_id, subtask_id) not executed.
problems.key_concept: Recursive CTE to generate all expected subtask numbers (1..N), then anti-join with executed.

hints.L1: First generate all expected (task_id, subtask_id) pairs. Then find which aren't in Executed.
hints.L2: Recursive CTE: anchor = (task_id, subtasks_count). Recursive: decrement subtask_id until 1.
hints.L3: WITH RECURSIVE gen AS (SELECT task_id, subtasks_count AS subtask_id FROM Tasks UNION ALL SELECT task_id, subtask_id-1 FROM gen WHERE subtask_id>1) SELECT g.task_id, g.subtask_id FROM gen g LEFT JOIN Executed e ON g.task_id=e.task_id AND g.subtask_id=e.subtask_id WHERE e.subtask_id IS NULL

problems.optimal_solution:
WITH RECURSIVE gen AS (
  SELECT task_id, subtasks_count AS subtask_id FROM Tasks
  UNION ALL
  SELECT task_id, subtask_id - 1 FROM gen WHERE subtask_id > 1
)
SELECT g.task_id, g.subtask_id FROM gen g
LEFT JOIN Executed e ON g.task_id = e.task_id AND g.subtask_id = e.subtask_id
WHERE e.subtask_id IS NULL;

problems.title: Report Contiguous Dates
problems.description: Find contiguous date ranges in Failed and Succeeded tables. Report period_state, start_date, end_date.
problems.key_concept: Gaps-and-islands with two period types. UNION ALL both, apply ROW_NUMBER grouping per state.

hints.L1: UNION ALL Failed and Succeeded with state labels. Apply gaps-and-islands per state.
hints.L2: Island key: DATE - ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY state ORDER BY date). Same key = same island.
hints.L3: WITH all_dates AS (SELECT fail_date dt,'failed' state FROM Failed WHERE YEAR(fail_date)=2019 UNION ALL SELECT success_date,'succeeded' FROM Succeeded WHERE YEAR(success_date)=2019), grp AS (SELECT dt, state, SUBDATE(dt, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY state ORDER BY dt)) grp_key FROM all_dates) SELECT state period_state, MIN(dt) start_date, MAX(dt) end_date FROM grp GROUP BY state, grp_key ORDER BY start_date

db_concepts.title: Indexes
db_concepts.summary: B-tree index: O(log n) lookup, supports range queries. Hash index: O(1) lookup, equality only. Covering index: query satisfied entirely by index. Composite index: (a,b,c) — useful for (a), (a,b), (a,b,c) but NOT (b,c) alone.
interview_questions: When would you NOT add an index? (High write volume — every write must update the index)
What is a covering index and when does the query planner use index-only scans?
Why does a composite index on (a, b) not help a query filtering only on column b?
What's the difference between a clustered index and a non-clustered index?
db_concepts.key_insight: Indexes trade write performance for read performance. Profile before adding. Over-indexing slows writes.

db_concepts.title: Query Optimization
db_concepts.summary: EXPLAIN shows query plan. Key patterns: seq scan (avoid on large filtered tables), index scan, nested loop (bad at scale), hash join (good for large unsorted joins). N+1 problem: avoid per-row queries in loops.
interview_questions: How do you diagnose a slow query? What does EXPLAIN ANALYZE show?
What is the N+1 problem and how do you fix it?
When does the query planner ignore your index? (low cardinality, function on indexed column, type mismatch)
db_concepts.key_insight: Avoid functions on indexed columns in WHERE. Use JOINs instead of correlated subqueries. Maintain stats with ANALYZE.

db_concepts.title: Transactions and ACID
db_concepts.summary: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability. Isolation levels: READ UNCOMMITTED → READ COMMITTED → REPEATABLE READ → SERIALIZABLE. Higher isolation = more locking = less throughput.
interview_questions: What are the 4 isolation levels and which anomalies does each prevent?
What is a phantom read? Dirty read? Non-repeatable read?
How does optimistic locking differ from pessimistic locking?
db_concepts.key_insight: Default: READ COMMITTED. REPEATABLE READ prevents non-repeatable reads but not phantoms. SERIALIZABLE prevents all anomalies but kills throughput.

db_concepts.title: Sharding and Partitioning
db_concepts.summary: Horizontal sharding: split rows across nodes by key. Vertical partitioning: split columns. Strategies: range (dates), hash (even distribution), list (regions). Hotspot: popular shard overloaded.
interview_questions: How would you shard a users table for 1 billion users? What key?
What problems does sharding introduce? (cross-shard joins, distributed transactions, rebalancing)
What is consistent hashing and why does it reduce reshuffling?
db_concepts.key_insight: Choose shard key to distribute load AND avoid cross-shard queries for common access patterns.

db_concepts.title: Replication
db_concepts.summary: Leader-follower: writes to leader, reads from followers. Sync: wait for follower ACK (strong consistency, higher latency). Async: don't wait (lower latency, risk of data loss on failover).
interview_questions: Sync vs async replication tradeoffs?
What is read-your-writes consistency? How to implement with replicas?
How does leader election work in Raft?
db_concepts.key_insight: Async replication trades durability for latency. Most real systems use semi-sync: ACK from at least one replica.

db_concepts.title: CAP Theorem
db_concepts.summary: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance — pick 2 (but P is mandatory in distributed systems, so really CP vs AP). Eventual consistency: replicas converge eventually. Strong consistency: all reads see latest write.
interview_questions: Where does PostgreSQL vs Cassandra vs DynamoDB sit on CAP?
What is eventual consistency? Name 3 systems that use it.
What is linearizability vs serializability?
db_concepts.key_insight: CP (PostgreSQL, HBase): sacrifice availability on partition. AP (Cassandra, DynamoDB): sacrifice consistency.

db_concepts.title: Connection Pooling
db_concepts.summary: Opening a DB connection is expensive. Pools reuse connections. Pool size: CPU_cores × 2 + disk_spindles (HikariCP). Too many connections = context switching. Too few = queuing. PgBouncer for PostgreSQL at scale.
interview_questions: What happens without connection pooling at scale?
What is the right pool size? How do you tune it?
What is a connection leak? How to detect and fix?
db_concepts.key_insight: 1000 app servers × direct connections = connection exhaustion. Use a pool proxy. Pool size ≠ thread count.
